1: šopĕṭēnî YHWH kî ʾănî bĕtummî hālāktî (šop̄ĕṭēnî YHWH kî ʾănî bĕṯummî hālāḵtî): 'Vindicate me, O LORD, for I have walked in my integrity.' Psalm 26 is a protestation of innocence — a genre related to the oath of clearance in which the accused declares their innocence before God (cf. Job 31). The verb šāpaṭ ('judge, vindicate') is a legal request: not punishment but examination and exoneration. The phrase bĕtummî hālāktî, 'I have walked in my integrity,' uses tōm ('completeness, wholeness') — the same word as Psalms 25:21. The accompanying confession ûbaYHWH bāṭaḥtî lōʾ ʾemʿāḍ, 'and in the LORD I trust without wavering,' frames the integrity as covenant-grounded, not self-generated.
2: bĕḥānēnî YHWH wĕnassēnî ṣārĕpâ kilĕyôtay wĕlibbî (bĕḥānēnî YHWH wĕnassēnî ṣārĕp̄â ḵilĕyôṯay wĕlibbî): 'Prove me, O LORD, and try me; test my heart and my mind.' The three verbs — bāḥan ('examine'), nāsâ ('test, try'), and ṣārap ('smelt, refine') — all describe metallurgical or judicial investigation. The objects kilĕyôtay ('my kidneys/reins' — the seat of deep feeling) and libbî ('my heart' — the center of will and thought) encompass the whole inner person: both emotion and cognition. The psalmist invites the most thorough possible divine inspection, confident that the examination will vindicate rather than condemn.
3: kî ḥasdĕkā lĕneged ʿênāy wĕhithallaktî bĕʾamittekā (kî ḥasdĕḵā lĕnegeḏ ʿênāy wĕhiṯhallakṯî bĕʾămiṯṯeḵā): 'for your steadfast love is before my eyes, and I walk in your faithfulness.' The phrase lĕneged ʿênāy ('before my eyes') — used elsewhere for what one constantly sees and is shaped by — means the psalmist's vision is constituted by YHWH's ḥesed: the divine love is his constant reference point. The hitpael wĕhithallaktî ('I walked about / I conduct myself') from hālak suggests habitual, lifestyle-level behavior — not isolated acts but the ongoing pattern of walking in ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth).
4: lōʾ-yāšabtî ʿim-mĕtê-šāwĕʾ (lōʾ-yāšabtî ʿim-mĕtê-šāwĕʾ): 'I do not sit with men of falsehood.' The negative lōʾ-yāšabtî ('I have not sat') followed by the parallel lōʾ ʾābôʾ ('I will not go in') with ʿim nĕʿālamîm ('with those who hide themselves/dissemblers') describe separation from the morally compromised. The company one keeps shapes the character one has: the psalmist's integrity is maintained partly by the associations he avoids. mĕtê-šāwĕʾ ('men of falsehood/vanity') and nĕʿālamîm ('those who cloak themselves, hypocrites') are the two groups he refuses to inhabit.
5: śānēʾtî qĕhal mĕrēʿîm wĕʿim rĕšāʿîm lōʾ ʾēšēb (śānēʾṯî qĕhal mĕrēʿîm wĕʿim rĕšāʿîm lōʾ ʾēšēb): 'I hate the assembly of evildoers, and I will not sit with the wicked.' The verb śānēʾ ('to hate') is strong — not merely avoid but actively reject. The noun qāhāl ('assembly, congregation') used for mĕrēʿîm ('evildoers') is a deliberate anti-type to the qāhāl of YHWH's worshippers (Psalms 26:12). The two assemblies — the evildoers' and the righteous — are mutually exclusive: one cannot inhabit both. The psalmist's claim to innocence rests partly on sustained non-participation in the alternative community.
6: ʾerḥaṣ bĕnikkāyôn kappāy wăʾăsōbbĕbâ ʾet-mizbaḥăkā YHWH (ʾerḥaṣ bĕnikkāyôn kappāy wăʾăsōḇḇĕḇâ ʾeṯ-mizḇaḥăḵā YHWH): 'I wash my hands in innocence and go around your altar, O LORD.' The verb rāḥaṣ ('to wash') applied to the hands is a ritual purity gesture, but here deployed metaphorically for moral innocence (bĕnikkāyôn, 'in cleanness/innocence'). The Pilate parallel (Matthew 27:24) uses the same gesture in reverse: he washes his hands to disclaim responsibility while the crowd accepts blood-guilt. The psalmist's hand-washing is a claim; Pilate's is a denial. The verb sābab ('go around, encircle') describes the processional circumambulation of the altar — active participation in worship, not passive attendance.
7: lĕhašmîaʿ bĕqôl tôdâ ûlĕsappēr kol-niplĕʾôtêkā (lĕhašmîaʿ bĕqôl tôḏâ ûlĕsappēr ḵol-nipl̂ĕʾôṯeḵā): 'proclaiming thanksgiving aloud, and telling all your wondrous deeds.' The two infinitives — lĕhašmîaʿ ('to cause to be heard') and lĕsappēr ('to recount, tell') — describe the content of the worshipper's participation: thanksgiving (tôdâ) and narration of niplĕʾôt ('wonders, marvelous deeds'). The tôdâ was both a thanksgiving offering and the liturgical speech accompanying it. The psalmist's worship is verbal proclamation — witness embedded in liturgy.
8: YHWH ʾāhabtî mĕʿôn bêtekā ûmĕqôm miškan kĕbôdekā (YHWH ʾāhaḇtî mĕʿôn bêṯeḵā ûmĕqôm miškan kĕḇôḏeḵā): 'O LORD, I love the habitation of your house and the place where your glory dwells.' The declaration ʾāhabtî ('I love') — qal perfect, completed and ongoing — applied to mĕʿôn bêtekā ('the dwelling of your house') expresses the psalmist's deepest affection as being for the sanctuary. The phrase mĕqôm miškan kĕbôdekā, 'the place where your glory dwells,' uses the theological kābôd — the radiant divine presence that filled the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34-35) and temple (1 Kings 8:10-11). Love of the sanctuary is love of the presence it houses.
9: ʾal-tʾĕsōp ʿim-ḥaṭṭāʾîm napšî wĕʿim-ʾanšê dāmîm ḥayyāy (ʾal-tʾăsōp ʿim-ḥaṭṭāʾîm napšî wĕʿim-ʾanšê ḏāmîm ḥayyāy): 'Do not sweep away my soul with sinners, nor my life with bloodthirsty men.' The verb ʾāsap ('to gather, sweep away') in negative imperative form — ʾal-tʾĕsōp ('do not gather away') — pictures divine judgment as a gathering-and-sweeping of the wicked. The psalmist's prayer: do not include me in that sweep. The phrase ʾanšê dāmîm ('men of blood') designates murderers and the violent; their distinguishing characteristic is bloodshed. The psalmist's protestation of innocence throughout the psalm is precisely the ground for this request.
10: ʾăšer bîdêhem zimmâ wîmînām māleʾâ šōḥad (ʾăšer bîḏêhem zimmâ wîmînām māleʾâ šōḥad): 'in whose hands are evil devices, and whose right hands are full of bribes.' The noun zimmâ ('evil plan, wickedness') describes premeditated wrongdoing — schemes carried in the hands. The right hand (yāmîn) māleʾâ šōḥad, 'full of bribes,' corrupts the justice system: the šōḥad perverts the judge's verdict, converting justice into commerce. This is precisely what Psalms 15:5 and Psalms 24:4 identified as disqualifying from divine presence — and why the psalmist protests his own innocence: his hands are ʾerḥaṣ bĕnikkāyôn (Psalms 26:6), not full of bribes.
11: waʾănî bĕtummî ʾēlēk pĕdēnî wĕḥonnēnî (waʾănî bĕṯummî ʾēlēḵ p̄ĕḏēnî wĕḥonnēnî): 'But as for me, I walk in my integrity; redeem me and be gracious to me.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but as for me') creates the contrast with the bloodthirsty of Psalms 26:9-10. The confession bĕtummî ʾēlēk ('I walk in my integrity') returns to the psalm's opening claim (Psalms 26:1) — inclusio formed by the same language. The paired imperatives pĕdēnî ('redeem me!') and ḥonnēnî ('be gracious to me!') place the psalmist's need entirely in YHWH's hands: integrity does not earn rescue; it gives YHWH a basis to act in covenant faithfulness.
12: raglî ʿāmĕdâ bĕmîšôr bĕmaqhēlîm ʾăbārēk YHWH (raḡlî ʿāmĕḏâ bĕmîšôr bĕmaqhēlîm ʾăḇārēḵ YHWH): 'My foot stands on level ground; in the great assembly I will bless the LORD.' The phrase raglî ʿāmĕdâ bĕmîšôr, 'my foot stands on level ground,' uses mîšôr ('level plain, evenness') — the same root as yāšār ('upright'). The foot that walked in integrity (Psalms 26:1) now stands on level ground — a spatial metaphor for moral stability. The vow bĕmaqhēlîm ʾăbārēk YHWH, 'in the great assembly I will bless the LORD,' returns the psalm to the sanctuary of Psalms 26:8: the individual's protestation of innocence culminates in corporate worship. Integrity leads to praise.
